name: Procept
classification_notes: Described by the foundry as experimental and exciting, with no conventional classification indicated; the display category fits given the context of bold, thrill-seeking experimental design.
summary: An experimental display typeface from Delve Fonts, positioned as a bold and adventurous design for the thrill-seeking user.
description: Procept sits within Delve Fonts' experimental Procepts5 range, a set of typefaces the foundry describes as exciting and unconventional. Little technical detail is available, but the offering is positioned for users willing to take risks with type choices. The lack of weight and language information suggests a limited, single-style release.
foundry_blurb: Delve Fonts describe Procept as part of their exclusive Procepts5 collection, aimed at bold users who enjoy thrill and delight, presenting it as an exciting and experimental design for those keen to be early adopters.

name: Tuppence
classification_notes: Tuppence uses reversed contrast, a hallmark of Victorian-era display type, derived from the Stephenson Blake typeface Blackfriars. Its ornamental features, catch words, swash capitals and wide proportions place it firmly in the display category.
summary: A reinterpretation of the Victorian reversed-contrast typeface Blackfriars, refined by Roberto de Vicq de Cumptich with wider proportions and additional weights for contemporary display use.
description: Drawing from the Stephenson Blake foundry's Blackfriars, de Vicq de Cumptich reworked the original into a more graceful, geometrically proportioned design suited to modern applications. The family runs across six weights from ExtraLight to Bold, each with a matching italic, and a variable font covers the full weight axis in both upright and italic. An extensive suite of ornaments, swash capitals, catch words and ligatures extends its usefulness into food and beverage packaging, poster work and restaurant branding.
foundry_blurb: Delve Fonts describe Tuppence as inspired by the Stephenson Blake typeface Blackfriars, reinterpreted by Roberto de Vicq de Cumptich to be more useful to contemporary designers. The redesign widens the lowercase proportions for a more geometric look, refines optical weight distribution, and adds a rich collection of ornaments, swash capitals, catch words and ligatures. Named one of I Love Typography's favourite fonts of 2021, Tuppence is positioned for display applications such as food and beverage labels, greeting cards, posters and restaurant logos.

name: 1403 Vintage Mono
classification_notes: A monospaced typeface with a single weight, explicitly modelled on the IBM 1403 mainframe line printer output, placing it firmly in the mono category with a retro/vintage character.
summary: A monospaced typeface drawn from the output of IBM's 1403 mainframe line printer, with a Pro variant covering 435 languages across Latin, Cyrillic, Greek and Hebrew scripts.
description: Designed by Jeff Kellem after Mark VandeWettering raised the question of whether a digital font existed that recalled the IBM 1403 line printer, it began with just 48 glyphs from the original 'A' chain and was gradually extended through research into modified chains. The Limited edition covers the basic Latin complement with small capitals standing in for lowercase; the Pro edition expands to 366 Latin-based languages plus Cyrillic, Greek and Hebrew, and includes OpenType features such as alternative fractions, stylistic alternates, historical forms and an extensive set of symbols and dingbats. Suited to display use on covers and film titles, and at text sizes it reads naturally in columns and grids where an official or archival tone is wanted.
foundry_blurb: Delve Fonts describe 1403 Vintage Mono as inspired by the IBM 1403 mainframe line printer from the 1960s, designed by Jeff Kellem after the idea was sparked by a public query about whether such a digital font existed. Research into original printer chains guided the design, with the Pro version supporting 435 languages and the Limited version offering a more focused Latin character set, both recommended for display use on covers and film titles as well as text settings requiring authenticity and officialdom.

name: Vena Amoris
classification_notes: A formal calligraphic script drawing on 19th-century penmanship traditions, designed to replicate the flowing letterforms of historical handwritten correspondence.
summary: A formal calligraphic script inspired by 19th-century New Orleans penmanship, with an extensive OpenType feature set including swashes, discretionary ligatures and smart roman numerals.
description: Designed by Kathryn Podorsky and inspired by a letter penned by her great-great-grandfather in 1895, the typeface channels the flowing elegance of period handwriting into a digital format. Its Latin-based character set covers 310 languages and is accompanied by contextual and stylistic alternates, swash variants, oldstyle figures and calligraphic word forms driven by OpenType logic. The name refers to Henry Swinburne's 1686 phrase for the vein reputedly running from the fourth finger directly to the heart.
foundry_blurb: Delve Fonts describe Vena Amoris as an endearing formal script with clever OpenType features, supporting 310 languages through an extensive Latin-based character set that includes discretionary ligatures, swash alternates, oldstyle figures, smart roman numerals, stylistic sets, and initial and terminal forms.

name: Tome Sans
classification_notes: Described by the foundry as combining appealing, discreet forms with versatility across multiple contexts, placing it in the humanist sans tradition with an emphasis on approachability and legibility rather than geometric rigidity.
summary: A ten-weight workhorse sans with extensive Latin language coverage and a variable font option, designed for everyday professional use.
description: Designed by Delve Withrington, Tome Sans runs from ExtraThin to Black with matching italics across 20 static styles plus a variable font with weight and slant axes. Over 860 glyphs cover 314 languages, and OpenType features include oldstyle and tabular figures, stylistic alternates, arrows and a full set of fractions. The Indigo Design Awards 2021 recognised it with a Silver award in Typography.
foundry_blurb: Delve Fonts describe Tome Sans as a large typeface intended for everyday use by everyone, combining appealing, discreet forms with useful features that perform both dutifully and beautifully across multiple settings. Rather than chasing design trends, they aimed for a timeless design with clarity, versatility and reliability as its core qualities.

name: Loniki
classification_notes: Loniki is a display typeface rooted in a 5th-century Greek inscription, combining rational geometry with sinuous curves drawn from late antique lapidary letterforms. Its blend of classical, Byzantine and gothic influences places it firmly in the display category with an inscriptional subcategory.
summary: A display typeface by Steven Skaggs drawn from a 5th-century Thessaloniki inscription, fusing classical, Byzantine and gothic letterforms across a 229-language glyph set.
description: Rooted in a specific lapidary inscription from Thessaloniki's archaeology museum, Loniki balances rational geometry with sinuous curves that recall the cultural crossroads of late antiquity. Extensive OpenType features, including discretionary ligatures and stylistic alternates, allow designers to shift the cultural register of a layout from classical to gothic without changing the font. A companion Symbols file adds over 140 ornaments, astronomical and alchemical devices, and Futhark runes. Winner of an Indigo Design Awards 2024 Gold in Typography.
foundry_blurb: Delve Fonts describe Loniki as inspired by a 5th-century inscription in Thessaloniki's archaeology museum, whose rational geometry and sinuous curves embody a blend of masculine and feminine, east and west, classical, Byzantine and gothic. The family supports 229 languages including Polytonic Greek, Coptic and Vietnamese, and ships with a Symbols companion containing more than 140 ornaments, devices and runes.

name: Quita
classification_notes: A connected calligraphic script drawn with a pointed pen, featuring confident curves, flourishes and swash capitals characteristic of formal penmanship traditions.
summary: A connected pointed-pen script drawn by calligrapher Xandra Y. Zamora, with elaborate swash capitals and flourishes suited to luxury and occasion work.
description: Penned by Xandra Y. Zamora, the script draws on her pointed-pen calligraphy practice, with swash capitals whose unexpected curved endings were reportedly inspired by a cat with a broken tail. OpenType contextual alternates handle spacing and rhythm automatically as you type, while stylistic alternates, discretionary ligatures and swash variants allow further customisation. Language support covers 312 languages across an extensive Latin Extended character set.
foundry_blurb: Delve Fonts describe Quita as a luxurious pointed-pen script by master calligrapher Xandra Y. Zamora, noting her confident curves and magnificent flourishes throughout. The typeface uses OpenType features applied automatically during typesetting to achieve harmonious rhythm, with additional stylistic alternates, ligatures and swash variants for further customisation. They position it for menus, wedding invitations, food packaging and luxury branding, with support for 312 languages.

name: Amador
classification_notes: A condensed English blackletter in the littera oxoniensis tradition, drawing on the calligraphic influences of Frederic Goudy and Rudolf Koch with Arts and Crafts movement sensibility. Designed explicitly as a display blackletter for headlines, logos and newspaper nameplates.
summary: A condensed English blackletter by Jim Parkinson, rooted in decades of newspaper nameplate work and the calligraphic traditions of Goudy and Koch.
description: Originally released in 2004, Amador draws on the littera oxoniensis tradition filtered through the Arts and Crafts movement and the influence of Frederic Goudy and Rudolf Koch. Parkinson refined the design over a prolific career producing blackletter nameplates for newspapers, giving the face a practical authority rare in revival blackletters. Version 2.2 expands the glyph set to over 1,000 glyphs across 336 languages, including Vietnamese, African and Oceanian scripts, alongside swash variants, ligatures, catchwords and OpenType fractions.
foundry_blurb: Delve Fonts describe Amador as primarily a condensed English blackletter, a littera oxoniensis shaped by the Arts and Crafts movement, with the works of Frederic Goudy and Rudolf Koch reflected in its forms. Originally published by Jim Parkinson in 2004, the typeface draws on his long career designing blackletter nameplates for newspapers. Version 2.2 expands glyph coverage to over 1,000 glyphs supporting 336 languages, adding stylistic alternates, swash variants, ligatures, catchwords, arrows, fractions and comprehensive OpenType feature support.

name: Baudot
classification_notes: Baudot is a grid-based display typeface with a marquee aesthetic, drawing on the visual language of Las Vegas entertainment signage. Its modular, geometric construction and headline-only orientation place it firmly in the display category.
summary: A grid-based display typeface originating from a 1968 design by François Robert, revived and expanded for the 21st century with a chromatic layering system.
description: Originally conceived in 1968 as a headline alphabet evoking Las Vegas marquee lettering, Robert's design circulated in the 1970s as dry transfer type under the name 'Chicago'. The digital revival, retitled Baudot and released in 2022, comes in three weights with OpenType features and over 900 glyphs covering 329 languages including Greek, Cyrillic and Vietnamese. A separate Baudot Pop subfamily, split into Open and Fill versions, allows chromatic layering effects for flashy headline treatments.
foundry_blurb: Delve Fonts describe Baudot as inspired by the bright lights of Las Vegas, originally designed by François Robert in 1968 as a headline alphabet evoking the glitzy entertainment of that era. The digital revival was produced in collaboration with Robert from 2022 onwards, reimagined with three weights, OpenType features including stylistic alternates and smart Roman numerals, a broad glyph set of over 900 glyphs supporting 329 languages, and a two-part Baudot Pop subfamily for chromatic layering effects.

name: Ritualist
classification_notes: Ritualist draws directly from 19th-century high-contrast wedge serif designs such as De Vinne, featuring sharp pointed terminals and dramatic thick-thin stroke contrast that place it firmly in the display serif tradition.
summary: A high-contrast wedge serif by Justin Penner that reworks 19th-century De Vinne forms through an overtly macabre sensibility, built across 18 styles with a variable font.
description: Inspired by De Vinne and similar 19th-century wedge serif designs, Ritualist amplifies the drama with infinitely sharp terminals and stroke contrasts that accelerate like a blade's edge. The family spans nine weights from Thin to Black, each with a matching italic, plus a variable font covering the full weight and slant axes. Over 1,000 glyphs provide coverage for 459 languages including Vietnamese and numerous African and indigenous languages, making it as practical as it is striking for display use.
foundry_blurb: Delve Fonts describe Ritualist as sharp as a knife and dangerously alluring, occupying a surreptitious space between beauty and darkness. Drawing on high-contrast wedge serif designs like De Vinne but viewed through a macabre lens, the design is defined by infinitely sharp points and dangerous curves. The foundry recommend it for headlines, titles and subheads where the brief calls for intrigue, darker desires or mystical encounters, and note it was named Steven Heller's Font of the Month for September 2025.

name: Fleisch
classification_notes: Fleisch is explicitly a blackletter family rooted in the brokenscript (gebrochene Schriften) tradition, drawing on German 1920s typefaces and historical lettering, with a simplified and partially romanised uppercase for legibility.
summary: A two-style blackletter family by Joachim Müller-Lancé, drawing on German childhood memories, 1920s typefaces and cigar-box lettering without adhering to any single historical model.
description: Fleisch comes in two distinct styles at matching weight and similar proportions: Wolf is pointed, slanted and traditional in feel, while Wurst leans squarer and chunkier. The uppercase is deliberately simplified and romanised across both styles to remain legible in all-caps settings, where ornate blackletter capitals typically break down. Each style ships with decorative initials, oldstyle figures, stylistic alternates for letters such as a, k and z, discretionary ligatures, and a small set of ornamental regalia.
foundry_blurb: Delve Fonts describe Fleisch as a blackletter pair inspired by the designer's childhood in Freiburg im Breisgau and by German 1920s typefaces, picture books, newspaper advertising and cigar box labels. Rather than following any single era or regional style, it borrows freely across sources. Fleisch Wolf is more pointy and elegant; Fleisch Wurst more square and earthy. The family supports 309 languages and includes features such as stylistic alternates, historical forms, oldstyle figures and discretionary ligatures.

name: Balboa
classification_notes: Balboa is a display gothic drawing on early sans serif and grotesque traditions, combining narrow headline proportions with ranging uppercase midlines and sign-painter brush terminals. Its lineage from ATF Headline Gothic and Stevenson-Blake-inspired grotesques places it firmly in the American gothic display category.
summary: A display gothic by Jim Parkinson synthesising ATF Headline Gothic, a San Francisco Chronicle banner face and a Stevenson-Blake-inspired grotesque drawn for Newsweek's 1980s redesign.
description: Parkinson combined wedge-shaped brush terminals from sign-painting tradition with ink traps at angled joins, producing a typeface that reads as simultaneously historic and purposeful. The narrow proportions and ranging uppercase midline push it towards headline and display use, though the wider weights handle subheading work capably. Version 2.2 expanded the family to nine weights plus two condensed cuts, with 880 glyphs covering 331 languages including Vietnamese and Latin-based African scripts.
foundry_blurb: Delve Fonts describe Balboa as a display gothic steeped in rich typographic history, blending elements of early sans serif and grotesque types with contemporary forms. Jim Parkinson evolved the design from ATF Headline Gothic, a Chronicle banner face called Banner, and Newsweek No. 9, a Stevenson-Blake-inspired grotesque created for Roger Black's 1980s Newsweek redesign. The v2.2 expansion added Regular and SemiBold weights, broad language support for 331 languages, and OpenType features including discretionary ligatures, fractions and tabular figures.

name: Cabazon
classification_notes: Parkinson explicitly describes it as an informal blackletter, drawing on Rudolf Koch, Ross George and Samuel Welo for simplified, hand-lettered blackletter forms with a casual character.
summary: An informal blackletter by Jim Parkinson, first released in 2005 and drawn from mid-20th century lettering artists including Rudolf Koch and Ross George.
description: Parkinson simplified classic blackletter forms to retain the warmth of hand-lettering while giving the face a looser, more casual character suited to logos, food and beverage packaging and movie posters. His decades of experience designing blackletter newspaper nameplates informed the design. Version 3.0 expanded the glyph set to over 1,000 glyphs covering 336 languages, with stylistic alternates, discretionary ligatures and a full suite of OpenType features.
foundry_blurb: Delve Fonts describe Cabazon as an informal blackletter with swagger, designed by Jim Parkinson and first published in 2005. Parkinson researched legacy blackletter types, hand-lettered samples and early manuscripts, finding particular inspiration in the mid-20th century work of Rudolf Koch, Ross George and Samuel Welo. The updated v3.0 release adds over 1,000 glyphs supporting 336 languages, along with stylistic alternates, discretionary ligatures and expanded OpenType features.

name: Peasy
classification_notes: Low-contrast with open apertures and a gestural, soft quality; these traits, combined with a comfortable x-height and readability focus, place it firmly in the humanist sans tradition.
summary: A low-contrast humanist sans by Peter Cho, designed for extended reading and medium-sized display use with broad language support across 367 languages.
description: Six weights from Light to Black, each with an italic, make up the family. Open apertures, soft stroke endings and a generous x-height give it a readable warmth without becoming decorative. OpenType features are well stocked: small caps, oldstyle and tabular figures, schoolbook alternates, arrows, fractions and a wide range of currency symbols, with Vietnamese and numerous African and Oceanic languages included in its 367-language coverage.
foundry_blurb: Delve Fonts describe Peasy as a friendly, modern sans serif intended for extended reading and medium-sized display settings. They highlight its low contrast, open apertures and neutral yet refreshing personality with a gestural, soft quality, noting that its broad language support includes Vietnamese and a panoply of African and Oceanic languages, alongside lively OpenType features such as small caps, oldstyle numerals and schoolbook stylistic alternates.

name: Overpass
designer: Delve Withrington, Dave Bailey, Thomas Jockin, Alan Dague-Greene
classification_notes: Derived from the U.S. Federal Highway Administration's Standard Alphabets for Traffic Control Devices, Overpass carries the open, legible proportions of highway signage letterforms, placing it firmly in the humanist sans tradition.
summary: A free, open-source sans drawn from U.S. highway sign lettering, expanded with Cyrillic and Vietnamese support and a companion monospaced variant for developers.
description: Originally commissioned by Red Hat and later developed with Google Fonts sponsorship, Overpass v4.0 brings nine static weights from Thin to Heavy, each with an italic, plus variable font support across weight and slant axes. The design traces its letterforms back to the Federal Highway Administration's traffic sign specifications, with adjustments made for screen legibility and display use. Overpass Mono accompanies the proportional family with five weights tuned specifically for programming environments, and the combined family covers over 305 languages.
foundry_blurb: Delve Fonts describe Overpass as a free and open-source interpretation of the Standard Alphabets for Traffic Control Devices published by the U.S. Federal Highway Administration. Starting from the original drawing specifications, critical adjustments were made for screen performance and display-weight use. The v4.0 update introduced variable fonts, expanded Cyrillic, Vietnamese, Swahili, Zulu and Oceanic language support, and the companion Overpass Mono family of five weights designed for programmers.

name: Uppercut Angle
classification_notes: The design draws on comic book and sports logo lettering traditions rather than calligraphy or formal type, with muscular, angular brush-inflected shapes suited to display and identity use.
summary: A brush-inflected display face by Joachim Müller-Lancé, drawn from martial arts and sports logo lettering traditions and originally created for a San Francisco Krav Maga training centre.
description: The letterforms are economical and angular, referencing comics and sports insignia rather than literal brushwork, with a decisiveness that keeps them legible at small sizes as well as large. Version 1.3 expanded the glyph set substantially, bringing language support to 329 languages including Vietnamese and Oceanian scripts. OpenType features include standard ligatures, slashed zero, ordinals, fractions and ornaments.
foundry_blurb: Delve Fonts describe Uppercut Angle as decidedly analog, muscular but not bulky, with the fluid but determined movements of a boxer or martial artist. Originally developed for the identity of a San Francisco Krav Maga centre, the design was drawn from pencil sketches and is intended to evoke the simplicity and efficiency of sports lettering without being theatrical. Version 1.3 significantly expanded language support to 329 languages.

name: Maxular
classification_notes: Explicitly described as a rounded slab serif; all stroke terminals, serifs and junctions are rounded, placing it firmly in the rounded slab category rather than a traditional bracketed or geometric slab.
summary: A rounded slab serif family by Steven Skaggs, notable for its Rx variant developed through clinical trials to aid readers with macular degeneration.
description: Six years in development, the family splits into two branches: the standard Maxular in six weights with italics for everyday use, and Maxular Rx, a pair of upright cuts expressly designed for readers with macular degeneration. The Rx variant incorporates large x-heights, open letter spacing, built-in generous line spacing and conventional letterforms proven in two independent clinical trials to maintain reading speed under the visual distortions the condition causes. Both branches cover over 700 glyphs with support for 305 languages.
foundry_blurb: Delve Fonts describe Maxular Rx as the first typeface expressly designed and scientifically tested to address the visual distortions caused by macular degeneration, with development guided by a consultant with the condition and validated through two independent clinical trials. The broader Maxular family extends those rounded, open forms into six weights with italics for general use, each font carrying a character set of over 700 glyphs and support for 305 languages.

name: Continuo
classification_notes: Continuo is an all-caps display face built on a single-continuous-line construction principle, drawing on neon tube aesthetics; its structural concept and decorative intent firmly place it in the display category.
summary: An all-uppercase inline display face where every letterform is traced by a single, unbroken line, modelled on the structural constraints of neon tube signage.
description: Designed by Delve Withrington, Continuo applies one strict rule to every glyph: the stroke must form a single, continuous, uncrossed path. The result resembles the logic of bent neon tubing, where heavier strokes leave a hollow void at the centre rather than a solid fill. Despite its singular weight and concept, the family covers nearly 900 glyphs supporting 461 languages including Vietnamese and multiple African languages, with alternate letterforms, arrows, ligatures and an extended currency set.
foundry_blurb: Delve Fonts describe Continuo as a fascinating all-uppercase display typeface wherein each letterform is constructed with a single, continuous line, presenting challenges akin to working with neon tubing. Strokes heavier than the tube width leave an unfilled interior void, and paths are never crossed to avoid optical massing. The name derives from the musical term Basso Continuo. An updated Latin Plus glyph set covers almost 900 glyphs across 461 languages, with alternate letterforms available in the lowercase slots.

name: Benicia
classification_notes: Benicia traces its lineage directly to Nicholas Jenson's Renaissance types, interpreted with modern refinements; it sits firmly in the old-style serif tradition, informed by humanist proportions rather than the mechanical rigidity of ATF Jenson or Bodoni revivals.
summary: A text serif by Jim Parkinson drawn from Nicholas Jenson's Renaissance types, designed to soften the mechanical rigidity of ATF Jenson whilst retaining its historical character.
description: Jim Parkinson returned to the original Jenson types as his touchstone after two decades of type design, intending to create something less rigid than ATF Jenson, itself a mechanised interpretation that lost much of the warmth of the Renaissance original. The family comprises four styles: Medium and ExtraBold, each with a matching italic. Suited to books, magazines and long-form editorial work where a contemporary text face with old-style warmth is required.
foundry_blurb: Delve Fonts describe Benicia as a creative blend of typographic influences, rooted in Nicholas Jenson's historical types but refined with modern design features for contemporary text settings. Parkinson revisited work he and Roger Black began in the mid-1970s for Rolling Stone magazine, going back to the source to produce a face that is, in their words, brilliant in text settings.

name: Stenciletta
classification_notes: A monolinear stencil typeface constructed strictly from circles and straight lines at limited angles on a square grid, with characteristic slanted bridges parallel to downstrokes. Its geometric, engineered construction and stencil function place it firmly in the display category.
summary: A monolinear stencil family in Regular and Bold, built on circles and straights with distinctive slanted bridges that create a diagonal-stripe texture across the letterforms.
description: Designed by Joachim Müller-Lancé, Stenciletta evokes the aesthetic of engineer-drawn signage alphabets, constructed strictly from circles and straight lines on a square grid with no overshoot. Its bridges are slanted parallel to the downstrokes rather than horizontal, giving the type a consistent diagonal rhythm that sets it apart from conventional stencil faces. The family includes eight fonts: Regular and Bold each split into Left, Right and Solid variants, allowing two-colour layering effects by stacking the Left and Right cuts in contrasting colours.
foundry_blurb: Delve Fonts describe Stenciletta as a monolinear stencil type family evoking mechanical engineering, construction and cargo shipping. The slanted bridges run parallel to downstrokes, lending a diagonal-stripe texture, and the design is built strictly from circles and straights on a square grid without overshoot. The family's Left and Right variants allow chromatic layering, and eight fonts in total cover Regular and Bold across standard, split and solid versions.

name: Sketchnote
classification_notes: Sketchnote is a casual script typeface drawn directly from Mike Rohde's own handwriting and hand-drawn lettering, with a bouncy baseline and ink-spread texture that places it firmly in the informal handwriting script category.
summary: A hand-drawn script family created by Mike Rohde to accompany his book 'The Sketchnote Handbook', comprising text, display and dingbat subfamilies.
description: Born from the practical needs of illustrating a book, the family captures the ink-spread texture and natural irregularity of Rohde's own handwriting. Sketchnote Text uses over 240 OpenType contextual alternates per font to replicate the variation found in genuine handwriting, whilst Sketchnote Square provides a drawn, compressed companion for headlines. A dingbats font supplies icons, rules and arrows suited to sketchnote-style documents and presentations.
foundry_blurb: Delve Fonts describe Sketchnote as a typeface born of necessity, designed by Mike Rohde to illustrate his book 'The Sketchnote Handbook'. The family is made up of five fonts: Sketchnote Text in Regular, Bold and Italic; the compressed Sketchnote Square for headlines; and the playful Sketchnote Dingbats. Contextual alternates automatically cycle through more than 240 letter variants to mimic natural handwriting, and the warm ink-spread texture captured during scanning was intentionally preserved through digitisation.

name: Astra Nova
classification_notes: Astra Nova is a display typeface built from letterforms composed of smaller grid-based units, giving it a distinctly modular, pixel-like quality that places it firmly in the display category with a retro-Americana character.
summary: A digital revival of François Robert's 1969 modular display face, built from grid-based letterforms with a distinctly retro-American character.
description: Originally released as a dry-transfer rub-down type in 1969 under the name Astra, this revival by François Robert reconstructs the face's distinctive vertical overlapping grid structure with precision. The 993-glyph set covers 442 languages including Vietnamese, Greek and Cyrillic, and OpenType features include standard and discretionary ligatures, smart Roman numerals and stylistic alternates. Best suited to headlines, posters, book covers and drop caps where large scale lets the modular construction register.
foundry_blurb: Delve Fonts describes Astra Nova as a revitalized classic Americana typeface by François Robert, first introduced in 1969 in rub-down format. The digital version carefully reproduces the subtle vertical overlapping grid of the original while greatly expanding glyph support to 993 characters and 442 languages, with OpenType features including ligatures, fractions, stylistic alternates and smart Roman numerals.

name: Rieven
classification_notes: Rieven is rooted in the historical uncial tradition, a majuscule script form, though Skaggs adapted it to include lowercase characteristics and an italic derived from 15th-century humanist cursive. Its display/script heritage places it firmly outside standard serif or sans categories.
summary: A two-style uncial family by Steven Skaggs that pairs an uncial-inspired face with a companion roman, both accompanied by italics and a set of over 180 ornaments.
description: Rieven Uncial takes the historical majuscule uncial script as its starting point but introduces lowercase characteristics to make it practically usable in running text. A companion roman, Rieven Roman, shares many glyphs with the uncial but strips away most uncial forms in the lowercase for settings where more conventional letterforms are required. The family received a Certificate of Excellence in Type Design from the Type Directors Club in the 2010 TDC2 competition, and supports 335 languages across its five fonts.
foundry_blurb: Delve Fonts describe Rieven as an innovative uncial type family designed by Steven Skaggs, built to be versatile rather than merely decorative. The uncial draws on true historical uncial forms while retaining a lowercase feel, and its italic references the letra antigua cursiva of 15th-century humanist masters. Rieven Roman was developed as a companion for settings where conventional letterforms are preferred, and the family is rounded out by Rieven Ornaments, containing over 180 symbols, arrows and decorative elements.

name: Discourse
classification_notes: Discourse is a reverse-contrast display typeface directly referencing 19th-century wood type traditions, with thick horizontals and thin verticals characteristic of the genre, reinterpreted into a neo-modern form across layerable chromatic variants.
summary: A 24-font reverse-contrast display superfamily by Dave Bailey, built around layerable chromatic effects inspired by 19th-century wood type and Wild West ephemera.
description: Inspired by western film imagery and the song title that sparked the design, Discourse takes classic reverse-contrast wood type as its starting point and pushes it toward a neo-modern sensibility. The family spans two weights, Light and Heavy, across three widths, Narrow, Middle and Wide, with each base style accompanied by Shadow, Outline and Fill variants designed for layered chromatic composition. Small caps, stylistic alternates and standard ligatures are included, and the family covers 152 languages.
foundry_blurb: Delve Fonts describe Discourse as a type superfamily of 24 fonts first conceived when designer Dave Bailey heard a preview of the song 'Dust and Buried Bones: A Western Fantasy' by Opiate Eyes, which conjured images of gritty western films and the Oregon Trail. Though it references classic reverse-contrast wood type from the Wild West era, Discourse re-interprets conventional forms into something more uniquely neo-modern. The heavy weights maximise visual impact with tight spacing and dark colour, while the light weights shed the weight of horizontal strokes entirely. Every weight includes shadow, outline and fill versions for layered chromatic effects.

name: Helfa
classification_notes: Bracketed serifs, concave stems, wedge-shaped terminals and true drawn italics place Helfa firmly in the humanist serif tradition, with design decisions that prioritise text readability and a hand-crafted quality over geometric regularity.
summary: A text serif by Delve Withrington with bracketed serifs, concave stems and true drawn italics, aimed at scholarly and long-form reading contexts.
description: Helfa is a five-weight text serif with matching italics, each italic form individually drawn rather than slanted from the roman. Concave stems, subtly cupped serifs and wedge-shaped terminals give the typeface a hand-made warmth, while a generous x-height and moderate stroke contrast keep it readable at small sizes. Glyphs such as b, d, p and q are drawn independently to avoid the mechanical repetition of mirrored or rotated forms.
foundry_blurb: Delve Fonts describe Helfa as thoughtfully planned, lovingly drawn and carefully crafted, noting that its stems curve gently inward, its serifs taper at shallow angles, and its italics are true drawn forms distinct from the roman. The family supports 196 languages and includes features such as standard ligatures, fractions, ordinals and superscript.

name: Tilden Sans
classification_notes: Tilden Sans is a low-contrast, square-ish sans serif with a generous x-height and incised terminals on curvilinear strokes, placing it in the geometric sans tradition with humanist warmth.
summary: A square-ish, low-contrast geometric sans by Delve Withrington with incised terminals and broad multilingual coverage across seven weights.
description: Designed with a generous x-height and low stroke contrast, Tilden Sans achieves a friendly, no-nonsense character through incised terminals on its curvilinear strokes, distinguishing it from plainer geometric sans designs without complicating legibility. The family spans seven weights from Light to Black, with the middle weights suited to text sizes and the extremes pulling well at display scale. A variable font is available, and the glyph set of over 900 characters covers more than 200 languages.
foundry_blurb: Delve Fonts describe Tilden Sans as thoroughly contemporary, clean and ready for work — stylish and friendly without being fussy. The square proportions, low contrast and generous x-height are complemented by incised terminals on curved strokes, lending the face a measure of distinction. Seven weights from Light to Black, a variable font, and coverage of over 225 languages make it a practical choice across a wide range of settings.

name: Piston
classification_notes: Piston is a display typeface drawing on Victorian industrial aesthetics, combining heavy verticals and blackletter-influenced density with neo-classical serif terminals and finials. Its bold, decorative character and headline-oriented design place it firmly in the display category.
summary: A bold display typeface by Steven Skaggs that draws on the industrial aesthetics of the Victorian era, pairing heavy verticals with neo-classical serif details.
description: Designed by Steven Skaggs, Piston takes its character from the iron-and-steel confidence of the late Industrial Revolution rather than the florid ornamentation more commonly associated with Victorian type. Heavy vertical strokes contrast with finely crafted terminal serifs and finials, giving the face a severity suited to logotypes and editorial headlines. An extensive OpenType feature set includes contextual alternates, custom ligatures, stylistic sets and titling alternates, allowing letterforms to shift considerably when converted to outlines or set tightly.
foundry_blurb: Delve Fonts describe Piston as a typeface that captures the indomitable spirit of the Industrial Revolution, tempering the brute strength of heavy verticals and tight setting with neo-classical details such as neo-gothic verticality and finely crafted finials. Designer Steven Skaggs intended it to be tinkered with: convert to outlines, manipulate the strokes, pack it tight, and a headline becomes a logotype. Built-in OpenType features automatically substitute custom ligatures and alternates, with support for 331 languages.

name: Summa
classification_notes: Summa features bracketed, cupped serifs with a narrow width and elevated x-height, placing it firmly in the transitional serif tradition. The true italic and inline variant reinforce its classical roman spirit with a calligraphic sensibility.
summary: A narrow, elevated-x-height classical roman by Don Sterrenburg, accompanied by a true italic and a visually striking inline variant with swash capitals.
description: Drawn by pen-and-ink master Don Sterrenburg and faithfully digitised by Delve Fonts over several years, Summa pairs bracketed cupped serifs with a narrow width and high x-height for an economical, refined texture. The true italic is a genuine calligraphic adaptation rather than a mechanical slant. The Inline variant restructures the letterforms entirely, with strokes that appear to twist and overlap in a ribbon-like, near three-dimensional effect, augmented by a set of swash capitals for expressive display settings.
foundry_blurb: Delve Fonts describe Summa as Don Sterrenburg's first digital typeface, produced through years of close collaboration in which his pen-and-ink drawings on illustration board were carefully transformed into working font software. The foundry characterises the regular as lofty and elegant thanks to its classic roman spirit, bracketed cupped serifs, narrow width and elevated x-height, and the inline as a complete re-imagining of the regular's structure with a ribbon-like, almost three-dimensional quality and optional swash capitals.

name: Quara
classification_notes: Quara's square forms, slightly rounded terminals and stroke omissions near joins place it firmly in the geometric/techno display category, drawing on OCR and avant-garde technology aesthetics rather than humanist or grotesque traditions.
summary: A compact geometric sans with square forms and stroke omissions near joins, taking its aesthetic cues from OCR letterforms and technology design.
description: Designed by Delve Withrington from an abandoned custom project, Quara uses deliberate simplification of strokes at joins, a technique borrowed from early OCR type design, as both a functional and aesthetic device. The square-ish letterforms with lightly rounded terminals give it a clean, technology-inflected look suited to headlines, subheads and short passages. Coverage spans Basic Latin through Latin Extended-A with support for 152 languages.
foundry_blurb: Delve Fonts describe Quara as featuring reduced letterforms approved for human use, with its square forms and rounded terminals drawing on avant-garde technology and gadget culture. The stroke-omission technique, originally used to aid OCR recognition by early computers, is treated as an aesthetic choice reflecting the growing acceptance of unconventional letterforms. The face works well for headlines and subheads across web, games, magazines and posters, while remaining versatile enough for shorter text passages at normal sizes.

name: Richmond
classification_notes: Richmond is described as a geometric sans display family, with acknowledged debts to Metro, Erbar and Johnston's Underground type. Its primary orientation is headlines and display use, with both upright and italic widths plus condensed variants and ornamented capitals.
summary: A geometric sans family by Jim Parkinson, drawing on the Metro and Erbar tradition, built across 21 styles with condensed cuts and ornamented capitals for display use.
description: Designed by Jim Parkinson following years of bespoke newspaper sans work, Richmond takes the geometric tradition of Dwiggins' Metro, Erbar and Johnston's Underground type as its reference point. The family spans 21 styles, adding normal-width upright and italic cuts to the condensed weights Parkinson had typically been commissioned to draw for editorial clients. Ornamented capitals are included alongside case-sensitive forms, fractions and localised forms, making it flexible across both newspaper headlines and broader display applications.
foundry_blurb: Delve Fonts describe Richmond as a geometric sans with a rich typographic history, designed by Jim Parkinson after years of creating custom narrow sans typefaces for newspapers. Parkinson set out to produce a fuller family with normal widths in upright and italic, condensed weights for tight settings, and a set of ornamented capitals. Currently available in 21 styles, Richmond is noted for its roots in the Metro, Erbar and Johnston Underground tradition, and was used by the Staten Island Advance for headlines and display type during its development.

name: Oktal Mono
classification_notes: A monospaced typeface built on a strict octagonal grid with monolinear strokes and no curves, replacing all circular forms with faceted polygonal geometry. The geometric modular construction and fixed-width design place it firmly in the geometric mono category.
summary: A collaboration between type designer Joachim Müller-Lancé and graphic designer Erik Adigard, built on a strict octagonal grid that replaces every curve with 45-degree facets.
description: Every letterform is constructed on a square grid using only straight strokes and 45-degree diagonals, translating circular forms into octagons with no curves whatsoever. The concept originated as a design-philosophical experiment: Müller-Lancé tested systematically how reducing a circle to a square, hexagon, or octagon affects legibility at different sizes, settling on the octagon as the most workable solution for average text. The family comes in four styles across Regular and Bold weights with matching italics, and supports 152 languages.
foundry_blurb: Delve Fonts describe Oktal Mono as a modularly structured, monolinear, and monospaced type family. It originated as a collaboration between type designer Joachim Müller-Lancé and graphic designer Erik Adigard of M-A-D in Sausalito, California. Adigard proposed a typeface intentionally generic but with no curves, breaking expected curves into facets, while Müller-Lancé systematically explored what happens when circles are translated into squares, hexagons, or octagons, ultimately settling on an octagonal base built on a strict square grid with only 45-degree diagonals throughout.
